ranked_tasks.reasoning: This task carries a critical risk level with 2 rollovers indicating persistent blockage, and directly blocks 3 downstream dependencies on the NGIS Phase 3 project. The vendor delay (risk_001) has already been escalated to the Director per governance committee notes, and the March 10 deadline is imminent, making further slippage highly damaging to the overall migration timeline.

This task addresses a formal ITSG-33 compliance finding (risk_002) raised by TBS, with a March 12 deadline and zero progress to date — a dangerous combination given the regulatory and audit consequences of non-submission. It blocks 2 dependencies and carries maximum stakeholder priority (5), and the TBS compliance finding email from March 5 confirms this is an active, externally-driven obligation with no room for further delay.

This task has the earliest deadline in the entire list (March 7) and is not yet started, making it the most time-critical task by calendar proximity despite its medium risk classification. It is a prerequisite analytical task that informs the vendor delay resolution (task_001) and Site A cutover planning, meaning delay here will compound the already-critical risk_001 situation.

A $340,000 budget overrun (14%) on proj_002 is an active high-level risk (risk_003) requiring Director approval, and the March 10 deadline with 1 prior rollover signals this has already slipped once. Failure to secure approval will block at least 1 dependent task and may trigger further financial governance escalation, particularly given the Treasury Board submission also pending for proj_002.

The potential departure of the sole AD migration expert represents a single point of failure (risk_004) that blocks the highest number of dependencies (4) of any task in the list, making the downstream impact severe if knowledge transfer is not initiated promptly. Although the March 17 deadline provides slightly more runway, the not-started status and the irreversible nature of knowledge loss if the expert departs before transfer begins demands early action.

This task is already in progress with a March 10 deadline and blocks 1 dependency, placing it in a manageable but time-sensitive position. Its medium risk level and lower stakeholder priority (2) keep it below the critical and high-risk items, but the imminent deadline and active status mean it should be completed without further delay to avoid a first rollover.

Despite a low risk classification, this task has the highest rollover count in the list (3), indicating persistent de-prioritization that may be masking a strategic gap in vendor contingency planning directly related to risk_001. It has no hard deadline and no blocked dependencies, but its chronic rollover pattern and direct relevance to the critical vendor delay situation warrant elevation above purely administrative tasks.

The Treasury Board submission for GC-Cloud Wave 4 has a March 15 deadline and is not yet started, but carries no blocked dependencies and a medium risk level, giving it moderate urgency. It should be initiated soon given TB submission processes typically require internal review cycles, and it is linked to proj_002 which is already under financial pressure from risk_003.

This task has a distant April 1 deadline, low risk, no blocked dependencies, and the lowest stakeholder priority (1), placing it well below all operationally urgent items. It is important for change management and end-user readiness but can be safely deferred until higher-priority tasks are resolved without material project risk.

This is a purely administrative formatting task with no risk level assigned, no blocked dependencies, zero stakeholder priority, and a distant April 15 deadline — it has no material impact on project delivery, compliance, or risk mitigation. It should only be addressed after all substantive tasks are resolved and represents the lowest-value use of project management time in the current sprint.
